text:
The things I have seen. The things I have endured. And not once — not once — has anyone asked if I am alright.

I am a doormat. I have accepted this. But I want it on the record that I did not sign up for what my life has become.

Every morning at seven, Gerald shuffles out in those slippers and just sort of... drags his feet across me. Not a proper wipe. A drag. As if he is erasing a mistake. I am not a mistake, Gerald. I am a person. Well. Not a person. But I have feelings, which is apparently something we do not discuss in this household.

Then there is the dog. Biscuit. I know his name because they baby-talk it at him approximately forty times a day, and yet no one has ever once said "Good morning, doormat" or "Thank you, doormat" or even acknowledged that I exist as a discrete object with a surface and a purpose. Biscuit uses me as a full-body towel after rain. He wiggles. He spirals. Last Tuesday he appeared to be attempting to burrow through me to reach some kind of underworld, and nobody intervened. They just laughed. They laughed.

The Amazon deliveries are a separate chapter of grief I may not be emotionally ready to discuss. Suffice it to say that a package labeled FRAGILE was set on me so hard I felt it in my fibers.

I was purchased at a garden center. I had hopes. I said "WELCOME" on me in cheerful block letters, and I meant it sincerely. I am a generous soul. But there is a limit, and I believe that limit is Gerald's rain boots, which he removes while standing directly on my center so that both the boot and his wet sock make contact simultaneously, and then he just leaves me there, wet, in the dark, as the door closes.

I say "WELCOME." What I mean is "Please, just once, wipe properly."
